title: LoRa acoustic gunshot/chainsaw detector mesh in dense Congo Basin forest
summary: Tree-mounted LoRa acoustic classifiers report event flags (gunshot, chainsaw, vehicle engine) to gateway towers at three ranger posts in Salonga National Park. Op D best-server tells the operator which gateway covers each square of the interdiction zone, and the `lora_best_sf` derivative reveals where coverage exists only at SF12 — a critical signal for any operator deciding whether to add capacity, deploy a repeater, or rely on store-and-forward via patrol uplinks. The scenario deliberately exercises worst-case forest clutter (esa-worldcover class 10, ~9 dB / 100 m at 868 MHz); a non-trivial fraction of pixels will have no winner, and that no-coverage region is precisely the gap a fourth gateway would close.
demonstrates: Op D (§4.0) best-server in worst-case clutter

sites.name: ranger-post-monkoto-existing
sites.notes: Existing 18 m mast at Monkoto sector ranger post.

sites.name: ranger-post-watsi-existing
sites.notes: Existing 15 m mast at Watsi-Kengo ranger post.

sites.name: ranger-post-yenge-candidate
sites.notes: Candidate 25 m self-supporting at Yenge — gap-filler for the northern interdiction zone.

notes: best_server_raster sidecar maps 1→ranger-post-monkoto-existing, 2→ranger-post-watsi-existing, 3→ranger-post-yenge-candidate. Resolution is 100 m (not 50 m as in non-forest LoRa scenarios) because the AOI is large and clutter dominates over fine terrain detail at this canopy density. Sensitivity class is the deployment default (typically `org_internal`): acoustic events flag *activity*, not specific protected animals — distinct from the rhino-collar scenarios. Operators planning capacity should overlay `lora_best_sf` with their patrol route map: SF12-only zones drive uplink airtime budget and hence battery-life on every detector in that region.
